Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just... not feel like it. I usually ended up doing most of my work in bursts when I absolutely had to, like pulling long sessions before deadlines. I learn better by doing things hands-on anyway, so sitting down to read or watch lectures felt like torture most of the time. I'd often wander around my apartment with my notes or pace while trying to memorize things.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines more than any real schedule. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything course-related, and then suddenly I'd have three assignments due and I'd be up until 3am getting everything done. I tried setting regular study times at the beginning of the semester, but my anxiety would spike and I'd just avoid it altogether. It's easier to work when there's immediate pressure, even though I know that's not healthy.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd log in when I remembered something was due soon or when I got an email notification that made my stomach drop. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, scroll through quickly, and then close it because looking at everything I needed to do made me feel overwhelmed. The gamified task notifications actually helped a bit because they felt less threatening somehow.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd try to check it daily, but that would last maybe a week before I'd fall off again. The closest it came to routine was when the gamified elements were introduced—earning points or completing challenges gave me a tiny dopamine hit that made logging in feel slightly less like a chore. But even then, it wasn't consistent.
Q5: The gamified tasks were actually the most useful for me because they broke things down into smaller, manageable pieces. I also liked the discussion boards when people shared practical examples or real-world applications—that helped me understand concepts better than just reading theory. Videos were okay if they were short and showed someone actually doing something rather than just talking at me. I need to see things in action to really get them.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the longer reading materials and the supplementary resources. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment, I probably didn't look at it. The lengthy video lectures were the worst—I'd start them and zone out within minutes, so eventually I just stopped trying. I know I should have engaged more with that stuff, but my motivation was basically nonexistent and I couldn't force myself to care about things that weren't immediately necessary.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see exactly how it connected to something I needed to submit, or if it was interactive in some way. The gamified stuff worked because there was immediate feedback and a sense of accomplishment. If something was just passive consumption with no clear payoff, my brain would check out completely. I also valued activities where I could move around or do something with my hands while learning.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they didn't exist, honestly. My brain would categorize them as 'nice to have but not happening' and move on. Required activities at least had the threat of consequences, which was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my fog of apathy. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe later' and later never came. The only exception was when optional activities were gamified—then they felt more like a game than work.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download PDFs and read them on my tablet while walking on my treadmill at home. I also used YouTube videos from other educators who explained things in ways that clicked better for me. Sometimes I'd meet up with classmates and we'd work through problems together on a whiteboard—that hands-on collaboration helped way more than staring at a screen alone.
Q10: There were definitely times when I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't know. Like when I'd print out practice problems and work through them at a coffee shop with my headphones in, or when I'd explain concepts out loud to myself while cooking dinner. I also had study sessions with friends where we'd quiz each other, but none of that involved logging in anywhere. My best learning happened when I was physically doing something.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture slides or readings once and then never open the LMS version again. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could access it without internet or without having to navigate through the platform. Once something was on my device, I could annotate it, reorganize it, whatever I needed. The LMS was just a delivery mechanism for me, not where actual learning happened.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would get so bad that just thinking about coursework made me feel physically sick, so I'd avoid it entirely. I was also dealing with some personal stuff—feeling really sad and disconnected from everything—which made it hard to care about school. Those weeks my attendance dropped to almost nothing and I'd just exist in this fog.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, the LMS was the first thing I'd avoid. The interface felt overwhelming when my mental state was already fragile. I had extracurricular commitments too, and sometimes those felt more manageable than facing my coursework, so I'd prioritize them instead. Confusion was a big one—if I didn't understand something, I'd shut down rather than seek help because asking for help felt like admitting failure.
Q14: Oh, definitely. I'd sometimes open videos or readings and just stare at them without absorbing anything. My eyes would move across the words but my brain was somewhere else entirely, probably worrying about everything I wasn't doing. I'd technically be 'studying' for an hour but retain nothing. It was like going through the motions because I felt like I should, even though I knew it was pointless.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt too big or too important. The anxiety would build up around them until they felt impossible to start. I knew they were important—that's exactly why I couldn't face them. It's like the higher the stakes, the more paralyzed I became. I'd distract myself with easier tasks or just scroll on my phone, feeling guilty the whole time but unable to stop.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage with the LMS at all. I'd operate in crisis mode, logging in frantically as due dates approached. The countdown would trigger enough adrenaline to override my avoidance. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have completed anything. It's not a healthy way to work, and it definitely contributed to my stress levels, but it was the only system that actually got me to produce work.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a brief boost and sometimes motivated me to engage more with the next assignment. But critical feedback, even when constructive, would send me into a spiral of self-doubt that made me want to avoid the course entirely. I'd sometimes delay checking my grades because I was scared of what I'd see. The gamified feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid, which didn't work great for how my brain operates. I needed more flexibility and more hands-on components. When modules were released weekly, I'd fall behind quickly and then feel overwhelmed trying to catch up. The structure assumed a level of consistent engagement that I just couldn't maintain. The gamified elements helped break up the monotony, but overall the format felt like it was designed for someone else.
Q19: Having everything spread across different sections of the LMS made it harder—I'd lose track of where things were. Clearer pathways and more interactive elements would have helped me stay engaged. The gamified tasks were a bright spot because they made engagement feel achievable. But long blocks of text or hour-long lectures were engagement killers for me. I needed variety and movement, not passive consumption.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's trying really hard but struggling to produce quality work, or maybe someone who's anxious and keeps checking things without actually completing them. It could also be perfectionism—opening assignments repeatedly but never feeling ready to submit. Or maybe they're confused and searching for answers without finding them. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. I completed my assignments and did okay on exams, but my LMS activity was pretty low because I did a lot of learning offline. Some people just don't need to interact with the platform constantly to understand the material. I'd absorb what I needed, do the work, and not spend extra time clicking around. Efficiency can look like disengagement from the outside.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns—like when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on things. But they miss so much context. They can't tell if someone is actually learning or just staring blankly at a screen. They can't capture offline study, group work, or the emotional state behind the numbers. A log might show I watched a video for 10 minutes, but it can't tell you I was crying the whole time because I was overwhelmed.
Q23: Being engaged, to me, means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just completing tasks—it's being mentally present and curious. I don't think I was truly engaged most of the time, even when I was getting the work done. Real engagement would mean I wanted to be there, not that I was just surviving until the semester ended.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how much effort it took just to submit assignments that seemed simple on paper. It wouldn't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the relief mixed with exhaustion after finishing something. It also wouldn't show the learning I did while moving around, talking with friends, or using outside resources. The data sees actions, not the person behind them.
